lines.greek: χεῖρʼ ἕλε δεξιτερὴν καὶ ἐδέξατο χάλκεον ἔγχος,
καί μιν φωνήσας ἔπεα πτερόεντα προσηύδα·
χαῖρε, ξεῖνε, παρʼ ἄμμι φιλήσεαι· αὐτὰρ ἔπειτα
δείπνου πασσάμενος μυθήσεαι ὅττεό σε χρή.
ὣς εἰπὼν ἡγεῖθʼ, ἡ δʼ ἕσπετο Παλλὰς Ἀθήνη.
οἱ δʼ ὅτε δή ῥʼ ἔντοσθεν ἔσαν δόμου ὑψηλοῖο,
ἔγχος μέν ῥʼ ἔστησε φέρων πρὸς κίονα μακρὴν
δουροδόκης ἔντοσθεν ἐυξόου, ἔνθα περ ἄλλα
ἔγχεʼ Ὀδυσσῆος ταλασίφρονος ἵστατο πολλά,
αὐτὴν δʼ ἐς θρόνον εἷσεν ἄγων, ὑπὸ λῖτα πετάσσας,
καλὸν δαιδάλεον· ὑπὸ δὲ θρῆνυς ποσὶν ἦεν.
πὰρ δʼ αὐτὸς κλισμὸν θέτο ποικίλον, ἔκτοθεν ἄλλων
μνηστήρων, μὴ ξεῖνος ἀνιηθεὶς ὀρυμαγδῷ
δείπνῳ ἁδήσειεν, ὑπερφιάλοισι μετελθών,
ἠδʼ ἵνα μιν περὶ πατρὸς ἀποιχομένοιο ἔροιτο.
χέρνιβα δʼ ἀμφίπολος προχόῳ ἐπέχευε φέρουσα
καλῇ χρυσείῃ, ὑπὲρ ἀργυρέοιο λέβητος,
νίψασθαι· παρὰ δὲ ξεστὴν ἐτάνυσσε τράπεζαν.
σῖτον δʼ αἰδοίη ταμίη παρέθηκε φέρουσα,
εἴδατα πόλλʼ ἐπιθεῖσα, χαριζομένη παρεόντων·

interlinear: her-hand he-took, the-right, and received the-bronze spear,
and to-her speaking, winged words he-addressed:
'Hail, stranger; with us you-shall-be-welcomed; and-then afterwards,
of-dinner having-partaken, you-shall-tell what you-need.'
so having-spoken he-led-the-way, and-she-followed, Pallas Athena.
and-they, when indeed within they-were of-the-house lofty,
the-spear then he-set, carrying-it, against a-pillar tall,
of-a-spear-rack within, well-polished, where indeed other
spears of-Odysseus steadfast-hearted were-standing, many,
and-her to a-chair he-seated, leading-her, beneath a-linen-cloth having-spread,
a-fair one, richly-wrought; and-beneath a-footstool for-the-feet there-was.
and-beside-it himself a-seat he-placed, inlaid, apart-from the-others,
the-wooers, lest the-stranger, vexed by-the-din,
at-the-meal should-take-no-joy, among-overbearing-men having-come,
and-so-that him about his-father, departed, he-might-ask.
and-washing-water a-handmaid in-a-pitcher poured, bringing-it,
a-fair one, golden, over a-silver basin,
to-wash-with; and-beside-them a-polished she-drew-up table.
and-bread the-honoured housekeeper set-beside-them, bringing-it,
dainties many adding, giving-freely of-what-was-there;
literal: he took her right hand and received the bronze spear,
and he spoke and addressed her with winged words:
'Hail, stranger; with us you shall be made welcome, and afterwards,
when you have tasted dinner, you shall tell what you need.'
So saying he led the way, and Pallas Athena followed.
And when they were within the lofty house,
he carried the spear and set it against a tall pillar,
within a polished spear-rack, where many other spears
of steadfast-hearted Odysseus were standing.
He led her to a chair and seated her, spreading a linen cloth beneath,
a fair chair, richly wrought, and beneath was a footstool for the feet.
Beside it he set his own inlaid seat, apart from the others,
the wooers, lest the stranger, vexed by their din,
should take no joy in the meal, having come among overbearing men,
and so that he might ask him about his father who was gone.
Then a handmaid brought water for washing in a pitcher and poured it,
a fair pitcher of gold, over a silver basin,
for them to wash, and drew up a polished table beside them.
And the honoured housekeeper brought and set bread before them,
adding dainties in abundance, giving freely of her store;
translations.elizabethan:
He took her by the right hand, took the spear
of bronze from her, and spoke with winged words:
"Hail, stranger. Thou art welcome in our house.
When thou hast supped, then tell us what thou need'st."
So saying, he led on; and after him
came Pallas Athena. Within the lofty hall
he bore the spear and stood it by a pillar,
in the smooth rack where many another stood,
the spears of that steadfast man, Odysseus.
Then to a chair he led her, richly wrought
and fair, and spread a linen cloth beneath,
and set a footstool under. For himself
he drew an inlaid seat, apart from all
those suitors, lest the stranger, vexed by din,
should find no pleasure in the meal, being come
among such overbearing men; and too
that he might ask her of his father, gone.
A maid then brought fresh water in a ewer
of gold, and poured it o'er a silver bowl
to wash withal, and drew a polished board
beside them; and the grave housekeeper set
bread out, and dainties of her store besides.
translations.modernist:
He took her right hand. He took the bronze spear from her. Then he spoke.

"Welcome, stranger. You will be looked after here. When you have eaten, you can tell us what you need."

He led the way and Pallas Athena followed. Inside the high house he carried the spear to a tall pillar and stood it in the polished rack, where many other spears were standing. They belonged to Odysseus, who had a steady heart.

He brought her to a chair and sat her down. He spread a linen cloth over it first. It was a good chair, well made, and there was a footstool for her feet. He put his own seat beside her, away from the others. He did not want the noise of the suitors to spoil her meal. They were overbearing men. He also wanted to ask her about his father.

A servant brought water in a gold pitcher and poured it over a silver basin so they could wash. She drew a smooth table up beside them. The housekeeper brought bread and set out what she had.
translations.storybook:
He took her right hand in his, and lifted the heavy bronze spear from her, and said in the kindest voice: "Welcome, stranger! You shall be well looked after here. And when you have had some supper, you can tell us what you need."

Then he led the way inside, and Pallas Athena followed him. In the great tall hall he carried her spear to a pillar and stood it carefully in the polished rack, where a great many other spears were standing already. Every one of them had belonged to his father, brave Odysseus.

Then he brought her to a beautiful carved chair, and spread a linen cloth over it before she sat, and slid a little footstool under her feet. He pulled up his own seat right beside her, well away from all the suitors, because he did not want their dreadful racket to spoil her dinner, and because there was something he badly wanted to ask her about his father.

A maid came with water in a golden jug and poured it out over a silver bowl so they could wash their hands, and pulled a smooth table up beside them. And the good housekeeper brought them bread, and all sorts of nice things to go with it.

murray.text: So saying, he led the way, and Pallas Athena followed. And when they were within the lofty house, he bore the spear and set it against a tall pillar in a polished spear-rack, where were set many spears besides, even those of Odysseus of the steadfast heart.

Athena herself he led and seated on a chair, spreading a linen cloth beneath—a beautiful chair, richly-wrought, and below was a footstool for the feet. Beside it he placed for himself an inlaid seat, apart from the others, the wooers, lest the stranger, vexed by their din, should loathe the meal, seeing that he was in the company of overweening men;

and also that he might ask him about his father that was gone. Then a handmaid brought water for the hands in a fair pitcher of gold, and poured it over a silver basin for them to wash, and beside them drew up a polished table. And the grave housewife brought and set before them bread,

and therewith dainties in abundance, giving freely of her store. And a carver lifted up and placed before them platters of all manner of meats, and set by them golden goblets, while a herald ever walked to and fro pouring them wine. Then in came the proud wooers, and thereafter
